caveats: a: The occupation data cover all apparel manufacturing, not 315250 or the narrowed private-label subset.
a: Task exposure does not establish technically reliable substitution or payroll reduction.
a: Conventional mechanization is excluded unless AI is integral to perception, planning, or control.
rho: No representative 315250 AI-adoption survey was found.
rho: Adoption may relieve bottlenecks or increase service levels rather than reduce headcount.
rho: The frozen labor ratio is measured at ancestor NAICS 3152 rather than this six-digit industry.
e: A vendor's repeat-order claim establishes that the model exists, not its prevalence across the industry.
e: Some firms mix own-brand, blanks, and private-label revenue and may only be partly eligible.
e: The frozen n input is missing and must not be inferred from this eligibility estimate.
s5: Neither source isolates 315250, the narrowed lens, or the target EBITDA band.
s5: BizBuySell's clothing-and-fabric median cash flow was $100,000 in 2025, well below the lens.
s5: Because frozen n is missing, transferable-firm volume cannot be quantified even with this probability.
q: The public pricing example is a single provider and may involve offshore production despite US-based project support.
q: Commodity products and sophisticated customers likely capture more savings than differentiated, fast-turn, low-volume work.
q: The estimate excludes implementation costs and volume effects.
d5: The BLS projection is NAICS 315, not 315250 or the narrowed lens.
d5: The 2024-2034 output path includes trade, productivity, and product-mix effects, not just final demand.
d5: The high case depends on the narrowed subset gaining share in a contracting domestic industry.
o: Operator requirement is separate from domestic location or employment intensity.
o: Standardized garments are more automatable than delicate, high-mix, or fit-sensitive products.
o: Some customers could vertically integrate, but that is self-supply rather than software-only fulfillment.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business private-label or OEM supply, commonly quoted per style and unit as an all-in price covering purchased materials, construction, decoration, labels, packaging, and delivery, with sampling and repeat production runs.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 315250 combines own-account branded manufacturers and jobbers with full-package private-label/OEM suppliers. The lens is narrowed to the latter because recurring outsourced manufacturing for external customers is economically distinct from selling a manufacturer's own apparel product or brand.
lens.excluded_activities: Own-brand and direct-to-consumer apparel companies whose economics depend primarily on brand ownership, merchandising, or retail; jobbers that only market their own finished apparel; captive production; one-off bespoke tailoring; contractors working only on customer-owned materials; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market full-package, OEM, and private-label cut-and-sew manufacturers that buy fabric and repeatedly supply custom or customer-branded apparel to external brands, uniform programs, distributors, institutions, or other business customers.

a.bridge: BLS NAICS-315 employment and O*NET sewing tasks proxy the 315250 private-label/OEM occupation mix. Sewing-machine operators are 25,280 of roughly 74,000 apparel-manufacturing jobs, and their core work physically positions fabric, guides garments, and monitors stitches. ILO evidence places the greatest GenAI exposure in clerical work. I therefore give high exposure to quoting, specification parsing, design iteration, sourcing coordination, scheduling, sales support, and documentation, but low exposure to sewing, material handling, finishing, and physical inspection. The broad NAICS-315 mix may include more brand, design, and headquarters work than the narrowed lens, biasing exposure upward.
caveats: The occupation data cover all apparel manufacturing, not 315250 or the narrowed private-label subset.
Task exposure does not establish technically reliable substitution or payroll reduction.
Conventional mechanization is excluded unless AI is integral to perception, planning, or control.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit staffing distribution and task-level labor-hour study across at least 10 US private-label/OEM manufacturers, including present AI and automation use, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Full-package manufacturers have a larger digital coordination layer than contractors because they source fabric, help develop products, prepare samples, quote complete builds, and manage packaging and delivery. Even so, the physical conversion of purchased fabric remains the labor core, limiting wage-weighted AI exposure.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for broader US apparel manufacturing from $12.3 billion in 2024 to $10.1 billion in 2034, a -1.9% annual rate. Applying that rate for five years produces approximately 0.91. Private-label/OEM work could underperform if sourcing moves offshore, or outperform if low-MOQ launches, uniforms, speed-to-market, traceability, and short restocks gain share, so the range widens around the mechanical proxy.
caveats: The BLS projection is NAICS 315, not 315250 or the narrowed lens.
The 2024-2034 output path includes trade, productivity, and product-mix effects, not just final demand.
The high case depends on the narrowed subset gaining share in a contracting domestic industry.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price shipments and order-book data for 315250 split between own-brand, private-label/OEM, uniforms, wholesale blanks, and institutional customers, plus import-share changes, would materially improve the forecast.
d5.rationale: The current-service basket faces a declining domestic production baseline, but recurring custom manufacturing retains uses where buyers pay for responsiveness, specification control, small lots, packaging integration, or reliable repeat runs.

caveats: A vendor's repeat-order claim establishes that the model exists, not its prevalence across the industry.
Some firms mix own-brand, blanks, and private-label revenue and may only be partly eligible.
The frozen n input is missing and must not be inferred from this eligibility estimate.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 315250 LMM candidates classifying own-brand, wholesale blanks, uniform programs, private-label/OEM revenue, customer concentration, and repeat-order share would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: The official definition is manufacturing from purchased fabric and explicitly includes clothing jobbers that buy inputs, design, arrange production, and market finished apparel; much of the code is therefore product or brand economics outside the lens. A real but minority private-label/OEM subset repeatedly makes customer-branded goods: an illustrative provider offers repeat restocks and reports a 94% repeat-order rate. The range reflects uncertainty over how many firms, rather than how much revenue, follow that model.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) at ancestor 3152: labor compensation (L02) $2,458.5M / sectoral output (T30) $7,128.7M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.1525 (margins.json[31]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Food Processing EBITDA/Sales 15.25% (dominant subsector of NAICS 31)); revenue/employee $155,236 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 3152); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: Operator requirement is separate from domestic location or employment intensity.
Standardized garments are more automatable than delicate, high-mix, or fit-sensitive products.
Some customers could vertically integrate, but that is self-supply rather than software-only fulfillment.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of commercially reliable end-to-end autonomous sourcing, cutting, feeding, sewing, finishing, inspection, packaging, and rapid style changeover across representative garments would justify a materially lower estimate.
o.rationale: The narrowed service buys and transforms physical inputs, validates fit and construction, manages production, and delivers labeled and packaged garments. Software can automate portions of design, planning, sourcing, customer communication, and inspection, but deformable-fabric handling and multi-style sewing remain difficult; customers still need an accountable manufacturer even if its labor content falls.

caveats: The public pricing example is a single provider and may involve offshore production despite US-based project support.
Commodity products and sophisticated customers likely capture more savings than differentiated, fast-turn, low-volume work.
The estimate excludes implementation costs and volume effects.
q.change_evidence: Customer contracts and five-year quote histories showing open-book clauses, rebid frequency, unit-price changes, gross margin, order retention, and the pricing of lead-time or low-MOQ premiums would directly measure retention.
q.rationale: Private-label projects receive an all-in unit quote after style, materials, branding, quantity, and delivery are set; the illustrative provider says higher quantities reduce unit cost and quotes are time-limited. That gives customers repeated opportunities to negotiate productivity gains away. The operator can retain more where AI reduces sampling cycles, speeds launches and restocks, improves quality consistency, or enables low minimums rather than only lowering a visible bill of materials or labor component.

caveats: No representative 315250 AI-adoption survey was found.
Adoption may relieve bottlenecks or increase service levels rather than reduce headcount.
The frozen labor ratio is measured at ancestor NAICS 3152 rather than this six-digit industry.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after labor, error, lead-time, and adoption data from ERP, product-lifecycle-management, computer-vision inspection, and tech-pack AI deployments at US apparel manufacturers would materially narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Much of the exposed coordination work is already digital and can adopt assistants for tech-pack interpretation, design variants, quote preparation, sourcing, production planning, customer updates, and inspection records within five years. Realized implementation is constrained by fragmented systems, inconsistent customer inputs, short style cycles, fabric variability, low data scale, quality liability, and human approval requirements.

s5.bridge: Census research reports a 62.4% older-owner share in manufacturing employer businesses, while BizBuySell reports 21 completed sales in the broader clothing-and-fabric manufacturing category during 2025. I use these as indicators of succession pressure and an observable transfer channel, then discount heavily because many older owners do not sell, some firms close or transfer internally, and marketplace transactions skew far below the target EBITDA band. The age measure likely overstates near-term selling intent; the marketplace likely undercounts private deals while overstating relevance of microbusinesses.
caveats: Neither source isolates 315250, the narrowed lens, or the target EBITDA band.
BizBuySell's clothing-and-fabric median cash flow was $100,000 in 2025, well below the lens.
Because frozen n is missing, transferable-firm volume cannot be quantified even with this probability.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession-intent data for actual LMM private-label/OEM candidates, plus GF Data, Axial, DealStats, and sector-broker records of 315250 control deals in the $1-10 million EBITDA band, would materially move the estimate.
s5.rationale: Ownership aging creates potential supply, but volatile demand, inventory and working-capital needs, customer concentration, thin margins, and owner-linked product knowledge can frustrate a qualifying control transfer.

principal_driver: AI-addressable design-to-production coordination around repeat private-label orders.
principal_weakness: A heterogeneous code, shrinking domestic output, and no defensible frozen LMM firm count.

run_meta.title: Cut and Sew Apparel Manufacturing (except Contractors)

sources.supports: Defines the industry as making cut-and-sew apparel from purchased fabric and includes jobbers that buy raw materials, design and prepare samples, arrange production, and market finished apparel; distinguishes work on materials owned by others.
sources.title: Census Bureau profile: 315250 Cut and Sew Apparel Manufacturing (except Contractors)

sources.supports: Reports roughly 73,000-74,000 apparel-manufacturing jobs in spring 2026 and 25,280 sewing-machine operators in 2025, with cutting, inspection, and production-supervision occupations also listed.
sources.title: BLS Industry at a Glance: Apparel Manufacturing, NAICS 315

sources.supports: Describes physical core tasks including positioning items under needles, guiding garment parts, threading machines, monitoring stitches, and detecting malfunctions.
sources.title: O*NET: Sewing Machine Operators 51-6031.00

sources.supports: Finds clerical occupations retain the highest GenAI exposure and frames exposure as task-transformation potential, not immediate whole-job automation.
sources.title: ILO: Generative AI and Jobs — A Refined Global Index of Occupational Exposure

sources.supports: Projects apparel-manufacturing real output from $12.3 billion in 2024 to $10.1 billion in 2034, a -1.9% compound annual rate, and employment from 84,500 to 58,800.
sources.title: BLS Table 2.11: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Illustrates a full-package model with fabric guidance, sampling, labels, packaging, MOQ 30+, repeat restocks, all-in unit quotes, quantity discounts, quality checkpoints, and a self-reported 94% repeat-order rate.
sources.title: DesignTo Clothing: Custom Apparel Development and Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 21 completed clothing-and-fabric manufacturer sales in 2025, with median price $150,000, median revenue $414,000, median cash flow $100,000, and 287 median days on market.
sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report Data Tables: Full-Year 2025

sources.supports: Using Annual Business Survey data, reports manufacturing employer businesses with a 62.4% share in its older-owner analysis.
sources.title: US Census Bureau CES Working Paper 24-71: The Metamorphosis of Women Business Owners — A Focus on Age

sources.supports: States apparel automation remains challenging because fabrics are deformable and describes staged deployments requiring digital validation, interoperability, runtime verification, and workforce enablement.
sources.title: A Deployment Case Study in Robotic Apparel Automation

title: Cut and Sew Apparel Manufacturing (except Contractors)
